attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-week history of a gradually worsening headache. The headache is bilateral but mainly on the right side and described as being severe and pulsating. It is continuous with fluctuating severity, being worst on waking and coughing, not associated with photo- or phonophobia and partially relieved with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. A few days ago, she developed tinnitus, visual blurring, transient dimming of vision and episodic, then persistent diplopia. Her medical history includes previous investigation for infertility. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 32 kg/m2. Her visual acuity is reduced in the left eye, and she has diplopia which is more pronounced on lateral gaze.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most probable cause of this patient's presentation is idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). This condition is more common in females, especially those who are overweight or obese. Polycystic ovarian syndrome has a comorbid association with IIH. The patient presents with characteristic features of IIH: substantial to severe daily headache associated with features of intracranial hypertension (worse in the morning, on coughing). The patient also gives a history of visual blurring, transient visual obscurations, and horizontal diplopia (due to unilateral or bilateral cranial nerve VI palsy).

Brain tumours commonly present with headache and can cause cranial nerve palsies due to raised intracranial pressure or direct compression. Headaches may be the sole presenting symptom in 3–4% of brain tumours at a late stage. While the VI nerve palsy here could be due to a tumour, the combination of obesity, female sex, and the characteristic pattern of headache with visual symptoms makes IIH more likely.

Giant cell arteritis occurs in patients over 50 years of age with new onset headaches associated with systemic symptoms of fever, malaise, night sweats, insomnia, and loss of appetite. The age and clinical features are not consistent with this diagnosis.

Migraine presents as recurrent episodic throbbing or pulsating, moderate to severe, and often unilateral pain that lasts 4-72 hours with complete freedom between attacks. The headache may be associated with nausea, vomiting and/or sensitivity to light, sound or smell. The continuous nature of symptoms and presence of cranial nerve VI palsy make this diagnosis unlikely.

Venous sinus thrombosis can present with a similar pattern of symptoms, but they are usually of such severity that patients present very early rather than waiting four weeks. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis typically presents with sub-acute to rapidly progressive headache over a few days. While it can cause raised intracranial pressure and VI nerve palsy, the gradual onset and presence of other features typical of IIH make this diagnosis less likely.

The presence of a VI nerve palsy in this case is likely due to raised intracranial pressure, which can cause this finding regardless of the underlying aetiology. The diagnosis of IIH requires normal neuroimaging, normal CSF composition (except for raised pressure), and the exclusion of other causes.
learningPoints: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension typically presents with headache, visual symptoms, and cranial nerve VI palsy due to raised intracranial pressure.
Obesity and female sex are major risk factors for idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
Cranial nerve VI palsy can occur in any condition causing raised intracranial pressure and is not specific to the underlying cause.
references: Ahmad SR, Moss HE. Update on the diagnosis and treatment of idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Semin Neurol [Internet]. 2019
Kurtzke JF. Neuroepidemiology. Ann Neurol [Internet]. 1984 Sep 1
Ahmed F. Headache disorders: differentiating and managing the common subtypes. Br J Pain [Internet]. 2012 Aug

Ferro JM, Canhão P, Stam J, Bousser MG, Barinagarrementeria F. Prognosis of cerebral vein and dural sinus thrombosis: results of the International Study on Cerebral Vein and Dural Sinus Thrombosis (ISCVT). Stroke [Internet]. 2004 Mar
